tasks.title: Updated Project Plan
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Update your Miro board to align with what was presented at the end of winter quarter, and archive anything that's no longer relevant to the project
  • Establish a project plan to deliver the project in spring quarter
  • Adjust scope for final deliverables

 

Details

Migrate Your Miro Board

If your section has new Pod assignments, move your Miro board to the new assigned space.

 

Update the Project Plan in Miro

Clean up your Miro board to remove unnecessary or outdated content. 

Update your board to reflect the latest scope based on your learnings from winter quarter. Spring quarter is dedicated to iterating on your project by:

  • Testing with users and incorporating feedback into designs
  • Engineering additional features to enhance product capabilities
  • Refining the design and user experience

Steps:

  • Review and update Miro board. Pay special attention to the Project Research and Project Plan sections. 
  • Update Project Schedule. Reflect on development cycles, key features, milestone, and deliverables. 
  • Update User Testing Plan. Show what you intend to test with users, who you will test with, and how you will incorporate the feedback. 

Note:

  • This assignment is due at the end of the day on the first day of class (Friday). Use class time to work with your team and then discuss the updated plan during your pod check-in meeting, so you can finalize and submit by the end of class or shortly thereafter. 

 

For Credit

  • Update your Miro board
  • Upload screenshots reflecting the updated portions of the board
tasks.summary: Goals Update your Miro board to align with what was presented at the end of winter quarter, and archive anything that's no longer relevant to the project Establish a project plan to deliver the project in spring quarter

tasks.title: Add Your Capstone Project to Your Portfolio
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Reflect on your Capstone journey so far
  • Update your portfolio website to include a section about your in-progress Capstone project

 

Details

  • Refer back to your MVP presentation from winter quarter and your updated project plan from earlier in spring quarter
  • Update your portfolio to capture the goals, process, takeaways, and next steps associated with your Capstone project
  • Be sure to address both the team/project perspective (for example, which actions did you take as a team?) and the individual/skills perspective (for example, what were your distinct individual contributions to and learnings from the project?)

 

For Credit

  • Post a link to your updated portfolio website
  • Answer the following question in bullet point form: once your Capstone project is complete, which additional changes will you be making to the Capstone section of your portfolio?

tasks.title: Deep Dive Summary #1
tasks.description:
Goal

  • Prepare for and capture takeaways from Deep Dive session with instructor.

 

Details

Pre-meeting

  • Complete the Agenda section for the Deep Dive session in your Miro board.
  • The agenda should include open questions, challenges you're facing, and any aspects of the project that you would like additional guidance on.

 

During meeting

  • Capture notes in the Discussion Notes section.

 

Post-meeting

  • Document next steps and follow-up actions in the Actions Items section.

 

For credit

  • All members should be present. Notice of absence needs to be provided.
  • Post PDF of the corresponding Miro deep dive panel after the deep dive meeting and prior to the assignment due date.

tasks.title: Cross-Section Mixer Reflection
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Reflect on your experience at the cross-section mixer

 

Details

  • The day this assignment is due, all Capstone students will be meeting for a cross-section mixer, rather than in your usual section. Details will be provided in the Canvas announcement at the start of the week.
  • This is an individual assignment, and you must attend the mixer in order to complete it. (Otherwise, you will need to plan on completing the make-up option described at the bottom of this page.)
  • To complete the assignment, please answer the following questions:
    • What was the most useful piece of feedback you received, and how do you plan on using it for your own project? (2-4 sentences)
    • What was the most useful or interesting thing (tool, technique, technology, idea, resource, way of thinking, etc.) you learned about during the mixer, and why? Note that this could be useful to your project, or useful to you personally or professionally. (2-4 sentences)
    • What is one suggestion you have to improve the mixer format for next year's Capstone? What would make this a more valuable experience for you? (1-2 sentences)
  • We will provide time to draft and submit this assignment during the mixer itself. So as long as you attend class that day and use the provided time to submit this assignment, you shouldn't have to do any work on it after class.

 

For Credit

  • Submit your answers to the questions above in PDF format

 

 

Make-Up Option (Requires Advance Prep Work)

  • If you can't attend the mixer, you can complete this option for credit instead.
  • Note that because this will involve proactively reaching out to other students, you should plan ahead if you think you'll need to use this option.
  • Reach out to at least two other Capstone students (who are not on your team), and explain your Capstone project to them. Make sure to communicate your problem description, users, development status, and any other important context. To guide your discussions, consider the following questions:
    • What current challenges are you facing in your project?
    • If you're in good shape to complete your project on time, do you have any "stretch goals" you're considering that you'd like feedback on?
    • What other tools, technologies, or approaches have you considered? 
    • Considering your role on the project, and the type of career path you plan to pursue after graduation, how do you plan on framing your work on the project on your resume? Portfolio? During interviews? 
  • Then, for your reflection submission, answer the following questions:
    • What are the names of the students you talked to?
    • What was the most useful piece of feedback you received, and how do you plan on using it for your own project? (2-4 sentences)
    • What was the most useful or interesting thing (tool, technique, technology, idea, resource, way of thinking, etc.) you learned about during the conversations, and why? Note that this could be useful to your project, or useful to you personally or professionally. (2-4 sentences)
tasks.summary: Goals Reflect on your experience at the cross-section mixer Details The day this assignment is due, all Capstone students will be meeting for a cross-section mixer, rather than in your usual section. Details will be prov

tasks.title: Draft Product and Usability Testing Script
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Prepare draft product that is functional enough for user testing
  • Prepare draft usability testing script that a facilitator can follow to run a structured user testing session
  • Gather feedback to improve the usability testing script
  • Revise testing script based on feedback

Details

  • Each team will submit a link to the draft version of the product that will be used for usability testing, along with a draft of the usability testing script
  • If the draft product is not accessible on the public Internet and you are unable to generate a private link for the instructional team to view, please upload screenshots of the draft product instead
  • Your usability testing script should be detailed enough that any member of your team can facilitate a testing session
  • Your script should include the following:
    • Introduction: What you will tell participants at the beginning of the session
    • Pre-task questions: Brief questions to understand participants’ backgrounds
    • User tasks (at least 2-3): Each task should include:
      • A scenario that gives the participant a realistic reason to use the product
      • How you will define whether the participant successfully completed the task
    • Post-task questions: Open-ended questions to capture participants’ experiences
    • Facilitator notes: Logistics such as time estimates for each portion of the session and a place to indicate which team members served as facilitator and notetaker during each session

For Credit

  • Submit link to current draft version of product
  • Submit draft of usability testing script (PDF)
tasks.summary: Goals Prepare draft product that is functional enough for user testing Prepare draft usability testing script that a facilitator can follow to run a structured user testing session Gather feedback to improve the usabilit

  • Collect feedback and synthesize insights about how you'll improve the product

 

For Credit

  • Post PDF of the corresponding areas of your Miro board

tasks.title: Final In-Class Presentation
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Present your project at the all-section celebratory event, which will take place during normal class time in week 9
  • Clearly illustrate project context, problem, research, solution, and value
  • Demonstrate the final product

 

Details

Presentation

Each team will have 7 minutes to present their project, plus Q&A if time allows:

  • Present slide deck (4 minutes) and play demo video of final product (2-3 minutes)
  • Answer questions and get feedback (time permitting)

 

Slide Deck

The Final Presentation deck provides an overview of your project and illustrates key functionality. Use the Final Presentation Template to structure your presentation, but feel free to style the slides however you like.

 

For Credit

1.) Make sure you have completed the Final Slide Deck assignment by Wednesday, May 27, including uploading your slide deck to the all-section Google Drive folder

2.) Present your slides at the all-section celebratory event on Friday, May 29

Your presentation will be evaluated across the following criteria. Up to 1 point will be given for each (1 point = fully meeting criteria; 0.5 points = missing content).

  • Deck: Are the context, approach, and solution clearly presented?
  • Presence: Are all members present and have speaking parts?
  • Delivery: Is the delivery smooth and well rehearsed?
  • Demo: Does the demo clearly show the functionality of the final product?
  • Responsiveness: Is the team attentive and responsive to feedback/critique?

In extreme circumstances where a student does not participate in the group work, that student will receive 0 points for this assignment.
tasks.summary: Goals Present your project at the all-section celebratory event, which will take place during normal class time in week 9 Clearly illustrate project context, problem, research, solution, and value Demonstrate the final p

tasks.title: Final Product and Testing Protocol
tasks.description:
Goals

  • Demonstrate a working final product that meets the goals, functionality, and expectations established in the Capstone project plan

 

Details

  • The final product demonstrates a solution that directly and confidently addresses the problem statement and the needs of the target audience. The product is delivered according to the scope outlined in the Capstone project plan that you revised at the beginning of the quarter. 
  • We will test the product using the link and testing protocol that you provide. The testing protocol should cover each of your key features and describe which results indicate a successful test and which results indicate a failed test. If there are known bugs or other limitations, please mention those, including any corresponding workarounds.
  • If the final product is not accessible on the public Internet and you are unable to generate a private link for the instructional team to use, please speak with your section instructor to arrange a live or recorded walkthrough instead.

 

For Credit

  • Submit a link to the final version of your product
  • Submit your testing protocol in PDF format

Your final product will be assessed according to the following criteria. Up to 4 points will be given for each unless otherwise noted (4 points = fully meeting criteria; 3 points = some issues; 0 points = substantial issues/missing).

  1. Scope: Delivery of pre-established scope (4 points)
  2. Functionality: Fully functioning technical solution (4 points)
  3. Usability: Usable user interface for target audience (4 points)
  4. Utility: Useful functionality addressing the problem statement (4 points)

Individual participation in group work: 4 points for full participation or 2 points for partial participation. In extreme circumstances where a student does not participate in the group work, that student will receive 0 points for this assignment.

tasks.title: Final Handoff/Closeout Documentation (Self-Directed Teams)
tasks.description:
Goal

  • Document your project for smooth handoff if your project will continue beyond Capstone, whether through a subset of your team members, a future Capstone team, or the open source community
  • Document your project for safe closeout if your project will not be continued beyond Capstone

 

Details

Write a Handoff / Closeout Plan

Create a plan for what to do with your project after Capstone. There are three options for self-directed projects:
  1. Ongoing projects - Commercialization: Document your project and create a plan reflecting the next steps for transitioning to a commercial endeavor run by your current team members or individual members of your team. 
  2. Ongoing projects - Open Source: Document your project and create a plan reflecting the next steps for transitioning to project to a future Capstone team or the open source community
  3. Non-continuing projects: Document steps to safely shut down your project and handle data and infrastructure securely

---

 

1. Ongoing projects - Commercialization

If one or more team members are planning to continue your project beyond Capstone for commercialization or other purposes, you should consider it a new project/entity which you'll need to handoff to. This will be as if your new team had a sponsor (your new entity) and you are handing off to this new sponsor. Here are things you should do for this transition:

  • Create an agreement with your team members to clarify ownership and intellectual property. The agreement should state that those members who are not interested in participating in the project beyond Capstone are relinquishing and transferring their intellectual property rights in the project to those who are continuing the project. Consult instructors for other continuing ownership options. 
  • Consider forming a legal entity to transition the project to, if you don't have one already. You can get advice from the instructors or CoMotion about doing this. For more information about ownership and IP, consult the IP page in the Capstone handbook.
  • Notify stakeholders (any individuals, organizations, companies that have been engaged for user research or collaboration) that your project is being transitioned a new team, with a date that the transition will be effective.
  • Create a separate website for your new project. Add a notice with information about the new project, and add a link to the new website from the website you created for the iSchool Capstone project. (If your project doesn't have a website, you can skip this step.)
  • Shut down any systems that are automatically collecting user data. Create new systems to collect data.
  • Notify any users you collected data from that their data will be transferred to the new entity.

What to document. Write up a final project status document accounting for all of the above steps and submit to Canvas.

---

 

2. Ongoing projects - Open Source

Open sourcing your project is a great way for future teams in the iSchool or outside contributors to continue your project idea and efforts. All projects that will be made available as open source projects need to prepare final status info and documentation on how to continue the project.

You cannot open source a sponsored project, unless you get written approval from your sponsor to do so.

Do the following to prepare your project for open source:

  • Notify stakeholders (any individuals, organizations, or companies that have been engaged for user research or collaboration) that your project is being transitioned to open source, with a date that the transition will be effective.
  • Post a notice on your website about the project being transitioned to open source, with a date that the transition will be effective. (If your project doesn't have a website, you can skip this step.)
  • Shut down any systems that are automatically collecting user data.
  • Notify any users you collected data from that their data will be deleted, with a date the data deletion will be completed by.
  • Delete all collected data.
  • Shut down all cloud infrastructure (app code, APIs, external integrations, etc.). Then close all cloud accounts associated with the cloud infrastructure.
  • Convert the code repository to a public repository and add an appropriate open source license.
  • Add a README document in the public repository with clear and concise instructions on how others can continue working on the project. The readme should also include the URLs for your presentation deck and final product.

What to document. Write up a final project status document accounting for all of the above steps and submit to Canvas. In your document, be sure to include the URLs for your presentation deck, final product, and GitHub repository. (While these assets may still be evolving through the end of Capstone, the URLs should remain the same.) Also, provide the post-graduation contact info for at least one team member (e.g. their GMail address), in the event that a future Capstone team decides to continue the project and has questions that can't be answered from the handoff/closeout documentation.

---

 

3. Non-continuing projects

While the hope is that most self-directed Capstone projects will be continued through a subset of your team members, a future Capstone team, or the open source community, some projects might not be carried on in any form after Capstone. If your project falls into that last category, you should prepare shutdown and final status documentation.

Do the following to prepare your project for shutdown:

  • Notify stakeholders (any individuals, organizations, or companies that have been engaged for user research or collaboration) that your project is being shut down, with a date that the shutdown will be effective.
  • Post a notice on your website about the project being shut down, with a date that the shutdown will be effective. (If your project doesn't have a website, you can skip this step.)
  • Shut down any systems that are automatically collecting user data.
  • Notify any users you collected data from that their data will be deleted, with a date the data deletion will be completed by.
  • Shut down all cloud infrastructure (app code, APIs, external integrations, etc.). Then close all cloud accounts associated with the cloud infrastructure.
  • Delete all collected data.
  • Back up and then delete the code repository.

What to document. Write up a final project status document accounting for all of the above steps and submit to Canvas.

---

 

For Credit

  • Submit handoff/closeout documentation (PDF)
tasks.summary: Goal Document your project for smooth handoff if your project will continue beyond Capstone, whether through a subset of your team members, a future Capstone team, or the open source community Document your project for s

tasks.title: Final Handoff/Closeout Documentation (Sponsored Teams)
tasks.description:
Goal

  • Document your project for smooth handoff to your sponsor

 

Details

Sponsored Projects

All sponsored projects should be treated like a continuing project, even if your team does not plan to work on the project beyond Capstone. There should be an identified point-of-contact at the sponsor who will receive your handoff documentation. Sponsors must be included in discussions and formulation of the Handoff/Closeout plan.

Do the following to prepare your project to be continued by your sponsor:

  • Notify your sponsor that your project will be handed off to them with the expectation that they will continue working on the project.
  • Identify the project owner (someone not on your team) from your sponsor's organization.
  • Establish a clear timeline with the project owner for the delivery of the project and documentation. Set up at least 2 meetings:
    • Meeting #1 (ideally in weeks 2-3) to discuss timeline and contents of the documentation
    • Meeting #2 (ideally in weeks 7-8) to demo the final product and review the code and documentation
  • Document your project so that the project owner can refer to it for reference. Include summaries about:
    • Purpose of the project
    • Relevant research
    • Design and UX direction
    • Code
  • Post a notice on your project website about the project being continued, that it will have a new project owner at the sponsor organization, and how to get in contact with them. Make it clear on your website that your team isn’t working on the project anymore. (If your project doesn't have a website, you can skip this step.)
  • Notify any users you collected data from that their data will be retained, and give them an option to delete their data.
  • Transfer cloud infrastructure (app code, APIs, external integrations, etc.) ownership to your sponsor. If your sponsor doesn't identify someone in their organization to assume ownership of cloud infrastructure, then proceed with a shutdown of all cloud infrastructure.  
  • Transfer code repository ownership to your sponsor .

What to document. Document the details of the steps you completed above and submit to Canvas.

 

For Credit

  • Submit handoff/closeout documentation (PDF)
tasks.summary: Goal Document your project for smooth handoff to your sponsor Details Sponsored Projects All sponsored projects should be treated like a continuing project, even if your team does not plan to work on the project beyond C
